ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal chicken; mapped to the nearest parent class 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302757) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated entry for sambal chicken. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the nearest defensible anchor. The sambal sauce component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for sambal chicken or equivalent Malay chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of sambal chicken is approximately 450 g (1 plate), as commonly portioned at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Chicken — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sambal Chicken (cr_id F11020046) with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were originally tagged to a delivery platform source in the input; these have been re-anchored to HPB Foodo values where available and supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chicken, broiler, meat and skin, cooked, stewed — NDB No. 05064) for cholesterol and fatty acid fractions. Trans fat value of 0.04 g/100g is retained from the input as a plausible trace value consistent with USDA data for cooked chicken with oil; documented as trace. Vitamin C 3.33 mg/100g is consistent with chilli and onion content and is retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for high-protein low-carbohydrate chicken dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sambal chicken contains only 1.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making a formal GI measurement impractical. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (15 × 1.4 × 4.5) / 100 = 0.95, rounded to 0.9. This is a negligible glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 277 mg/100g from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) and added salt in sambal paste
hypertension.guidance: A 450 g serving delivers approximately 1247 mg sodium (over half the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore); patients with hypertension should request reduced belacan and avoid adding soy sauce, fish sauce, or other high-sodium condiments, and limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate 1.4 g/100g with high protein 22.88 g/100g and negligible sugar 0.12 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal chicken has a negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 0.9 per 450 g serving) and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, total meal carbohydrate must be accounted for when served alongside rice, noodles, or roti, which can contribute 40–80 g carbohydrate per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.22 g/100g is low; cholesterol 24.44 mg/100g is modest; sodium 277 mg/100g warrants portion control
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal chicken is relatively heart-friendly due to low saturated fat and moderate cholesterol, but the sodium content from belacan means individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g per meal and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean white meat with low saturated fat 2.22 g/100g; chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sambal chicken is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lean protein profile and low saturated fat, but the chilli and belacan content should be minimised during flares, and individuals sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine) should monitor their response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Chicken (Malay: Ayam Sambal; Chinese: 参巴鸡) is a popular Malay and Singaporean hawker dish consisting of chicken pieces stir-fried in a spiced sambal paste made from belacan (fermented shrimp paste block), dried or fresh chillies, onions, garlic, and cooking oil. The dish is characterised by its bold, pungent, and moderately spicy flavour profile, with the umami depth of belacan balanced by the heat of chilli and the sweetness of caramelised onion. The chicken is typically cooked until the sambal paste coats each piece thoroughly, resulting in a slightly sticky, richly coloured dish. Nutritionally, sambal chicken is high in protein (22.88 g/100g) and moderate in fat (12.09 g/100g), with very low carbohydrate content (1.4 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is moderately elevated at 277 mg/100g due to belacan, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a main dish at Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and Malay restaurants, typically accompanied by steamed white rice, nasi lemak, or roti. A standard hawker portion is approximately 450 g. The glycemic load per serving is negligible, making it an excellent protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.
common_user_queries: Is sambal chicken suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in sambal chicken?
How much sodium is in sambal chicken?
Can I eat sambal chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020046, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked chicken. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented. All multilingual names verified against standard Malay, Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi usage for this dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for sambal chicken is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo entry; estimated potassium ~290 mg/100g from chicken and onion components is noted in the input but not adopted as a confirmed value. 3. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and no approved secondary source provides a composite sambal chicken entry with full micronutrient breakdown. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100g is retained as a trace value consistent with cooking oil used in stir-frying; this is below the 0.1 g/100g threshold for 'trace' labelling under Singapore Food Regulations. 5. Cholesterol value of 24.44 mg/100g and vitamin C value of 3.33 mg/100g were originally attributed to a delivery platform source in the input; these have been re-evaluated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked chicken) and are plausible — cholesterol is consistent with skinless cooked chicken (~85 mg/100g for pure chicken, diluted by sambal paste components), and vitamin C is consistent with chilli and onion content. Both are retained with medium confidence. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ≈ 15, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for very-low-carbohydrate high-protein cooked chicken dishes; no direct GI measurement for sambal chicken exists in the database. 7. The input health_suitability references 'dosa' as an accompaniment, which is inconsistent with typical sambal chicken service context (rice or roti are more common); this reference has been removed from the output health context.
